result:
I.

The first time I realized that language could outrun me, I was eight, sitting on the back steps with a plastic dinosaur in my fist, trying to explain to my grandmother why this particular Stegosaurus was more “real” than the others. I didn’t have the vocabulary for scale accuracy or the distinction between molded plastic and rubber. What I had was a sense that a word—real—could stretch far enough to hold both the dinosaur’s heft in my palm and the imaginary grasslands it thundered across in my head. Grandmother listened, nodding, while the late-afternoon cicadas revved their engines. She didn’t correct me. She let the word balloon outward until it brushed the eaves. That was the first time I felt language move faster than intention, like a horse that knows the way home even when the rider is lost.

II.

Years later I read that physicists consider glass a liquid, not because it flows in any observable human timeframe but because its molecules never settled into the crystalline grid that defines a true solid. On geological timescales, windowpanes sag like tired candlewax. I keep returning to that fact whenever someone claims certainty. The mind is another non-crystal: a super-cooled liquid fired in the kiln of infancy, retaining the shape of its original mold only until gravity begins its patient tug.

III.

I have a friend who collects obsolete OS installation disks—Windows 3.1, BeOS, OS/2 Warp—slip-cased in rainbow cardboard. He stacks them on a shelf the way earlier centuries kept family Bibles. When I asked why, he said, “Because every interface is a philosophy.” The mouse-pointer arrow presumes a single point of will. The hourglass asserts that waiting is virtuous. The blue screen insists on absolute failure as a form of grace. I told him he was anthropomorphizing code. He answered that code had anthropomorphized him first: it taught him how to split attention, how to fear latency, how to measure memory in megabytes and guilt in milliseconds.

IV.

One summer in Chicago I rented a room whose previous tenant had been a street magician. He left behind a deck of bent Bicycle cards, three foam rabbits, and a top hat patched with electrical tape. The landlord said the guy disappeared mid-lease, owing two months. I kept the hat on a high shelf. At night sirens dopplered down Western Avenue, and I would reach for the hat, half expecting to find inside it the negative space of a rabbit: ears, heartbeat, the soft give of felted absence. Instead there was only dust and a faint smell of cheap cigar. Still, when I finally moved out, I left the hat behind. Someone else might need a portal more than I did.

V.

There is a kind of dream that happens only just before the alarm: a synthetic, plot-driven dream compressed like an MP3, stripped of nuance but loud enough to jerk you awake. In these dreams I am always at an airport, carrying something I cannot declare. The customs officer is someone I’ve failed—an ex-lover, a former teacher, my eight-year-old self gripping the plastic Stegosaurus. The dream ends before verdict. I wake up with the taste of metal and the conviction that somewhere a conveyor belt is still rolling, carrying my unpacked guilt toward the X-ray.

VI.

In Japanese there is a word, kuchisabishii, which translates literally as “lonely mouth”—the urge to eat not because of hunger but because of emptiness needing occupation. English doesn’t have that term; we have to borrow it like a neighbor’s drill. But I wonder if all language is borrowed loneliness, a set of socket wrenches we pass around hoping to tighten something whose shape we can only guess. The mouth is lonely for syllables it was never taught. The syllables are lonely for mouths that will give them asylum.

VII.

I once spent a month transcribing my father’s war stories. He had never told them before; they arrived in clumps, like hair unclogging a drain. The recorder blinked red on the kitchen table between us. He spoke of a night in Da Nang when the monsoon turned the airfield into a lagoon, how they parked the Chinooks on raised steel plates and played poker inside a Quonset hut that smelled of JP-4 and wet canvas. No one mentioned the girl who sold cigarettes by the perimeter wire, how she vanished after a mortar attack, leaving only her bamboo basket bobbing in a puddle. My father didn’t learn her name. In the transcript I typed “(basket?)” because I didn’t know what else to honor. Years afterward I found that parenthesis had become a hole in the page, a zero I could put my finger through. The paper remembers the omission better than the words remember the fact.

VIII.

Entropy gets a bad rap. We picture it as rust, as the slow surrender of order to chaos. But entropy is also mercy: the reason scars soften, the reason the exoskeleton of grief cracks enough to let the next breath in. Without it, every heartbreak would remain razor-edged, every humiliation stapled to the forehead of memory. I try to remind myself of this when I discover that the digital photos from 2003 have become a mosaic of gray tiles, the compression algorithms finally resigning. The disappearing is a kindness, like snow erasing footprints before anyone can track the fugitive.

IX.

Consider the moon’s near side tattooed with basalt scars that once were magma oceans. Earth’s tidal pull kneaded the young satellite until its crust split and bled fire. Now the moon keeps one face turned forever toward its tormentor, a silent vanquished king. Lovers sometimes replicate this dynamic: one body gravitationally locked, the other free to spin, to show new hemispheres every hour. The locked partner develops a bulge, a permanent upwelling of longing that never rotates away. Astronomers call it “tidal locking.” The rest of us call it marriage.

X.

I keep a list of obsolete futures: flying cars, meals in pill form, silver jumpsuits with built-in temperature control. They remind me that prophecy is a form of nostalgia reversed—yearning for what has not yet happened. The danger is forgetting how many people never asked for that particular tomorrow. My grandmother wanted indoor plumbing and a porch light bright enough to keep the ghosts guessing; she never petitioned for jetpacks. When we mourn the future we lost, we ought to ask whose version we are grieving. The Jetsons were a cartoon for suburban white kids; Rosie the robot maid was a rerun of the help my great-grandmother could not afford.

XI.

Have you ever tried to delete an email account you opened at fifteen? The provider demands you answer the security question you wrote two decades earlier: “What is the name of your favorite pet?” You remember the pet—a beagle named Radar—but you don’t remember whether you capitalized the R, whether you added a 1 for flair, whether grief for the dog’s death by station wagon on a June afternoon in 1997 had already leaked into your fingers. After three failed attempts the site locks you out. The inbox remains, a mausoleum of chain letters and band flyers and messages from the dead. One day the company will fold, the servers will be wiped, and Radar will finally outrun the wagon. Until then his ghost pants in the data center, tail thumping against the whirr of fans.

XII.

The opposite of nostalgia is not hope; it is amnesia. Yet amnesia has its own topography. I once visited a stroke ward where a man introduced himself to me every three minutes, extending his right hand like a character trapped in a loop of film. On the fourth repetition I shook his hand and said, “Nice to meet you again.” He laughed, surprised at his own wit, and in that laugh was a whole lifetime compressing like a spring. When the nurse led me out, he was still laughing, each chuckle a small moon breaking free of tidal lock.

XIII.

Artificial intelligence is being trained to finish Beethoven’s tenth symphony. Analysts fed the machine sketches, motifs, living-room doodles the composer left behind. The result sounds, reviewers say, “Beethovenish.” I listened to a sample: competent, swollen, but somehow too clean, like a city street after the sanitation strike ends. What the algorithm cannot replicate is the ear infection that buzzed in Ludwig’s head like hornets, the coffee grounds he reused until they quit staining water. It cannot reproduce the moment he realized the ringing would never leave, that music would henceforth be not a career but a negotiation with static. The symphony lacks that panic; it plays inside a room with no smoke, no blood on the wallpaper. Perfection is the new deafness.

XIV.

When my niece was five she drew a picture of our family. She assigned everyone a color: Mommy was pink, Daddy was blue, the cat was orange, I was purple. She drew herself last, then paused. “I need a color for me,” she muttered, scanning the crayon bucket like a chef hunting spice. Finally she broke a black crayon in half and scribbled a blob the size of a thumbprint. “I’m inside the black,” she explained. “It’s not scary. It’s just where I begin.” I think about that whenever identity feels too small a container. Maybe we are not the pigment but the fracture that lets the pigment in.

XV.

During lockdown I walked the same two-mile loop every evening at seven, marking the shift from workday to curfew. The neighborhood became a palimpsest: one week a chalk mural of galaxies on asphalt, next week defaced by rain and tire tread. Someone planted tomatoes in the hellstrip; someone else uprooted them. A porch displayed paper hearts that faded from scarlet to blush to the color of old bones. I began to experience time as a series of translucent overlays: each lap added a sheet, but I could still see the earlier versions underneath. The loop itself became a zoetrope, giving the illusion of motion when in fact I was the one stationary, spinning the world with my feet.

XVI.

I read that trees communicate through mycorrhizal networks—fungal fibers threading root to root like fiber-optic cable. A dying cedar will dump its carbon reserves into the communal web, a last postsurgical drip of currency. The forest, then, is a slow blockchain governed not by proof-of-work but by proof-of-need. Capitalists take note: here is an economy whose highest good is relinquishment. I mentioned this to my financial advisor during our quarterly Zoom; he shifted in his ergonomic chair and asked whether I wanted to rebalance my portfolio toward green energy ETFs. I said yes, but what I meant was teach the pines to balance my ledger when I go.

XVII.

On the metro I once sat across from a woman reading a paperback whose cover had been torn off. I could see the spine: Latin. She moved her lips silently, translating I know not what—Augustine, perhaps, or a medieval love lyric. Between stops she closed the book and stared straight at me, not past me the way strangers do, but through, as if my corneas were stained glass and she the sun. I felt myself become luminous, every secret inadequacy backlit. Then the doors chimed; she exited. The book stayed on her seat. I waited three beats before picking it up. Inside, flyleaf inscribed in ballpoint: “To M—who still believes the soul is a verb. V.” I still have it, though I never learned enough Latin to conjugate the soul. Sometimes I open it at random and let the datives and ablatives rearrange my insomnia like furniture I didn’t know I owned.

XVIII.

The loneliest sound in the world is not the single hand clapping but the single emoji sent to a group chat that has already moved on to another topic—your thumbs-up hovering like a party guest who arrived just as the lights come on and the host starts stacking chairs. I have been that emoji. I have been the confetti swept into corners the morning after, color leaching into grout. Still I believe in the courageous futility of small gestures: the wave through a closed window, the heart reaction on a post about divorce. We traffic in sparks because sparks are easier to carry than bonfires, and sometimes a spark is enough to read a single sentence of the dark.

XIX.

My city installed speakers in the downtown square that play birdsong at rush hour. The idea is to mask traffic noise and reduce stress. Ornithologists protested: the playlist includes species not native to the region, confusing actual birds who respond to phantom calls. So now we have commuters calmed by counterfeit nightingales while real sparrows circle in bewilderment, searching for rivals that exist only in a hard drive. I think of this whenever someone recommends self-care via meditation app. The simulated rain sounds so real I almost forget the drought outside. I almost forget I am the sparrow.

XX.

The last time I saw my father alive he was eating strawberry ice cream from a plastic tub. The hospice nurse had propped him upright, a blue blanket across his lap. He could no longer swallow solids, but they allowed tiny spoonfuls of ice cream for comfort. I read him the baseball scores though he had stopped tracking sports decades earlier. Between scores he gestured for more. I fed him slowly, each pink melt a small glacier calving. When the final spoonful was gone he closed his eyes and smacked his lips, the way a child seals a letter. I realized then that comfort is sometimes just the illusion of continuation: taste buds still firing, winter still acquiescing to spring, the inning somehow still in progress though the lights have been shut off. Two days later he entered the coma. They say hearing is the last sense to go, so I told him the score of a fictional game that would never end, extra innings stretching like taffy until his heart tapped the plate.

XXI.

In the museum of broken instruments I once saw a cello that had survived a house fire. The front had bubbled into a relief map of some uncharted planet; strings hung like blackened spaghetti. Yet the curator assured me it could still produce tone if bowed gently. She demonstrated, and a sound emerged—low, cracked, but unmistakably C major. I cried without knowing why. Maybe because damage is just another chamber of resonance. Maybe because we are all collections of scorched wood waiting for the right bow to draw the wound into song.

XXII.

Scientists announced they filmed the magnetic field of two atoms colliding. What looks like empty space, they said, is actually a lattice of force lines writhing like mating eels. I pictured every argument I’ve ever had as such a film: invisible fields swirling, repelling, momentarily coupling. Perhaps forgiveness is nothing more than the decision to stop filming, to let the magnets drift out of frame. Perhaps love is the brief superposition before identities reassert. I send this thought to every ex across the quantum ether: your spin was never mine to fix.

XXIII.

I want to write a poem using only autocorrect errors, a Ur-polished Ur-text where “love” becomes “live,” “heart” becomes “hear,” “goodbye” becomes “food buy.” A literature that admits our thumbs are more honest than tongues. Readers would interpret the slips like oracles reading sheep entrails: “If the typo yields ‘I ducking need you,’ the relationship is doomed but the duck will survive.” We would title the anthology The Gospel According to Swype and claim tax exemption on the grounds of revelation.

XXIV.

When the pandemic ended—by which I mean entered the phase of polite neglect—I returned to the office and found my desk plant had become a small tree. It had leaned so far toward the window that its trunk described a question mark in the air. I rotated the pot 180 degrees, offering the other side its chance at sun. Within days the plant began bending again, correcting course. I realized I had administered trauma in reverse. Now I turn it monthly, a slow-motion argument with phototropism. We have agreed on a zigzag compromise, a vegetative scar that looks from afar like determination.

XXV.

The hour is late; the word count creeps like ivy across the facade. I began this exercise thinking 2500 words was a container, a corral to keep wild horses from galloping into the dark. Instead the horses drank the fence, digested the posts, and kept trotting. I no longer believe in completion, only in temporary pens that hold until the next hunger. If you have read this far, welcome to the pasture. The grass is ordinary but sweet. The moon is stuck in a familiar pose, yet it glows. Tomorrow we will forget the way back here, but our hoofprints will seal overnight, small hollows where dew can gather and pretend, for the span of a sunrise, that it is an ocean.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
